hypothesis: A breakout above the upper Bollinger band with rising OBV and RSI above 50 signals a strong bullish trend, while a breakout below the lower band with falling OBV and RSI below 50 signals a strong bearish trend. Using ATR to set stop‑loss and take‑profit adapts risk to current volatility, and exiting when any two of the three factors reverse reduces exposure to false breakouts.

Combining a Bollinger breakout with a mid‑range RSI confirmation and an ATR‑based volatility filter limits entries to strong, high‑volatility moves, reducing whipsaws and improving risk‑reward balance.

A Bollinger breakout indicates a price move, RSI confirms momentum, and OBV ensures volume backs the move; together they reduce whipsaws in a 30‑minute time frame. ATR‑based SL/TP scale risk to current volatility, improving risk‑adjusted returns.

A Bollinger band breakout combined with a high‑momentum RSI filter captures only strong directional moves while the ATR‑based stop‑loss and take‑profit adapt to current volatility, reducing whipsaws in ranging periods.

A breakout above the upper Bollinger band is more reliable when accompanied by rising OBV and strong RSI momentum; the same logic applies to a downside breakout. This combination filters out false breakouts in choppy markets and aligns price action with volume, improving entry quality while ATR-based risk management scales stops and targets to volatility.
